era_context: The Western Roman Empire is experiencing the beginning of a major military crisis. Germanic tribes, particularly the Visigoths under Alaric I, are pressing on the empire's frontiers. Stilicho is consolidating power as regent for the young Emperor Honorius. The Eastern Roman Empire under Arcadius is relatively more stable but also faces Germanic pressure. In China, the Eastern Jin dynasty is in decline. The Sassanid Persian Empire dominates the Middle East and Central Asia.

events.title: Alaric I's invasion of Italy begins
events.description: The Visigothic king Alaric I invaded Italy, crossing the Alps and threatening the heartland of the Western Roman Empire. This invasion marked the beginning of a major military crisis for Rome. Alaric's forces ravaged northern Italy, and the Roman general Stilicho was forced to respond militarily. The invasion was not immediately repelled; Alaric would remain a threat to Italy for nearly a decade, eventually sacking Rome in 410 CE. The invasion of 401 CE demonstrated the fragility of Roman military defenses and the inability of the empire to prevent Germanic incursions into its core territories.
events.certainty_note: Alaric's invasion of Italy in 401 CE is attested in multiple sources. Claudian's account is contemporary and detailed. The date and basic facts are well-established, though interpretations of the invasion's causes and significance vary.

events.title: Stilicho's military response to Alaric's invasion
events.description: Following Alaric's invasion of Italy in 401 CE, the Roman general Stilicho mobilized Roman forces to confront the Visigothic threat. Stilicho withdrew troops from the Rhine frontier and other defensive positions to concentrate forces in Italy. This strategic decision left other frontiers vulnerable and contributed to the later Germanic invasions across the Rhine (406 CE). Stilicho's military response to Alaric was initially successful, culminating in the Battle of Pollentia in 402 CE, but the long-term strategic consequences of weakening other frontiers were severe.
events.certainty_note: Stilicho's military response to Alaric's invasion is attested in sources, but the exact strategic decisions and their consequences are interpreted differently by modern scholars. Claudian's account is contemporary but propagandistic.
cross_references: 406-001 (Rhine crossing, consequence of Stilicho's strategic choices)

events.title: Honorius assumes greater personal authority as Western Roman Emperor
events.description: By 401 CE, the Western Roman Emperor Honorius, who had been a minor when he ascended to the throne in 395 CE, was reaching adulthood and beginning to exercise greater personal authority, though Stilicho remained the dominant military and political figure. Honorius was born in 384 CE and would have been approximately 17 years old in 401 CE. His reign (395–423 CE) was marked by military crises, the loss of Roman control over much of the Western Empire, and increasing dependence on Germanic military commanders. The period of 401–406 CE represents the transition from Stilicho's dominance to the final collapse of Western Roman imperial authority.

sources.name: Claudian, De bello Gothico and other panegyrics

events.certainty_note: Honorius's age and the transition to greater personal authority are documented in sources, but the exact nature and extent of his authority in 401 CE are uncertain. Stilicho remained the dominant figure in practice.
cross_references: 406-002 (Stilicho's fall, leading to Honorius's greater personal authority)

disconfirming_evidence: The causes of Alaric's invasion of Italy in 401 CE are debated by modern scholars. Some argue that Alaric was seeking to negotiate a settlement with Rome rather than to conquer territory. Others suggest that Alaric was responding to internal pressures within the Visigothic confederation or seeking to establish a power base within the empire. The characterization of the invasion as a 'barbarian invasion' may be anachronistic; Alaric was a Roman military commander (magister militum) and may have been operating within the framework of Roman military politics. Stilicho's strategic decision to concentrate forces in Italy at the expense of other frontiers is sometimes criticized as a strategic error, but other scholars argue it was a rational response to the immediate threat.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 401 CE is heavily dependent on Claudian's panegyric, which is a contemporary but explicitly propagandistic source. Later sources (Orosius, Zosimus) are compilations that may contain errors or distortions. The perspective of Alaric and the Visigoths is entirely absent from surviving sources; Alaric is portrayed as an external enemy, but he was in fact a Roman military officer with complex motivations. The sources are biased toward the imperial court and military elite; the experiences of the civilian population, provincial populations, and non-elite groups are not documented. Events in other regions of the world are essentially absent from Western historical records. The characterization of this period as a 'barbarian invasion' reflects modern historiographic categories that may not accurately represent the complex political and military dynamics of the era.

graph_edges.note: Alaric's invasion of Italy in 401 CE led to Stilicho's military response and the Battle of Pollentia in 402 CE.
Stilicho's decision to concentrate forces in Italy in 401 CE left the Rhine frontier vulnerable, contributing to the Germanic crossing in 406 CE.
Alaric's invasion of Italy in 401 CE was the beginning of a process that culminated in the sack of Rome in 410 CE.
